entities.name: Auto-apply tools
entities.context: Tools that automatically submit job applications on behalf of job seekers; V considers them career self-sabotage

entities.name: Metric fixation bias
entities.context: Cognitive bias where people feel productive due to vanity metrics (e.g., application counts) despite poor outcomes like 0.2% response rates

entities.name: Hidden job market
entities.context: V's belief that 70% of jobs are filled through hidden networks, not public postings

entities.name: Arms race dynamics
entities.context: Auto-apply tools create an escalating cycle where employers raise barriers in response to spam, harming the entire job market ecosystem

entities.name: Vanity metrics
entities.context: Misleading productivity signals (application volume) that auto-apply tools exploit to retain desperate users

entities.name: Auto-apply companies
entities.context: Companies that build and sell auto-apply tools; V believes they know their tools don't work and don't care

entities.name: Job seekers
entities.context: Desperate individuals exploited by auto-apply tools who harm themselves and the broader market

entities.name: Employers
entities.context: Hiring entities who raise barriers in response to spammed applications from auto-apply tools

relationships.context: Auto-apply tools exploit metric fixation bias to make users feel productive while delivering near-zero results
Auto-apply tools generate vanity metrics (application counts) as a substitute for actual hiring outcomes
Every spammed application gives employers excuses to raise barriers, creating a market-wide arms race
Auto-apply tools target public postings, but 70% of jobs are filled through hidden networks — making the tools structurally misaligned
Companies that build these tools know they don't work and literally don't care about user outcomes
Desperate job seekers use auto-apply tools, harming themselves and the broader ecosystem
Spammed applications from auto-apply tools give employers justification to raise hiring barriers
